FOREWORD
The 1954 General Assembly imposed upon the Legislative
Research Commission the duty of furnishing the printer correct
copy of bills and resolutions for inclusion in the Kentucky Acts .
House Bill No. 440 of 1964 added the requirement that the
Legislative Research Commission prepare indexes to
alleditions
ofthe Kentucky Acts after proofreading from the enrolled bills
.
This volume represents the fulfillment
ofthese obligations
.
This edition
ofthe Kentucky Acts continues the practice
,
initiated
in1962
,
ofcarrying new material
initalics and deleted
material
inbrackets when
abill amends
anexisting section
of
the Kentucky Revised Statutes
. By this procedure
, members
of
the General Assembly and other interested persons may readily
determine changes
inexisting law without constant referral
to
the Kentucky Revised Statutes
.
Inthe preparation
ofthis volume
ofthe 1964 Kentucky Acts
the Secretary
ofState has furnished access
tocopy
offiled en-
rolled bills and resolutions
. The staff
ofthe Legislative Research
Commission compared the prepared page proofs with the enrolled
bills
. The staff
isespecially grateful
toSecretary
ofState Thelma
Stovall and Mrs. Lucille Barclay
ofher staff for their cooperation
inmaking the files
ofthe enrolled bills available despite the fact
that
itmust often have interferred with their office routine
. In
order
toexpedite furnishing copy
tothe printer
, bills and resolu-
tions are inserted by chapters according
tothe order
inwhich
they received gubernatorial action and were filed
inthe office
of
the Secretary
ofState
. Mr. Courtenay
J.Walker
, Administrative
Assistant
tothe Director
, has had primary responsibility for the
preparation
ofthis volume
.
Frankfort
, Kentucky
June
1, 1964
HARRY LEE WATERFIELD
Chairman
JAMES
T.FLEMING
Director

# INDEX

The index is a valuable tool for finding specific information in a book. It is usually located at the end of the book and lists the topics covered in the book along with the page numbers where they can be found.

The index is organized alphabetically by topic. This makes it easy to find the information you are looking for.

The index is a great way to review the material in a book. It can help you to remember the key points of the book.

The index is also a great way to find information on a specific topic. If you are looking for information on a specific topic, you can use the index to find the page numbers where that topic is discussed.

The index is a valuable tool for students, researchers, and anyone who wants to find information in a book.

## WATERFORD

The city of Waterford is located in the southeastern part of Ireland. It is the oldest city in Ireland and was founded by the Vikings in the 9th century.

Waterford is a popular tourist destination and is known for its beautiful architecture, history, and culture.

The city is home to a number of museums, galleries, and other cultural attractions.

Waterford is also a major port city and is an important center for trade and commerce.

The city is located on the River Suir and is connected to the rest of Ireland by a network of roads and railways.

Waterford is a vibrant and dynamic city with a rich history and a bright future.

---

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the Department of Finance and the Governor.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 45.150 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the approval process for state-funded construction, alteration, reconstruction, or repair projects exceeding twelve thousand dollars. It requires submission of plans, specifications, cost estimates, and bids to the Department of Finance for approval. The Department of Finance shall not approve contracts if the cost exceeds the appropriation, the work is insufficient for its purpose, or cash is unavailable in the State Treasury. Capital outlays not exceeding twelve thousand dollars require approval of plans and release of appropriation by the Governor, who must ensure plans align with legislative intent and cash availability. This subsection does not apply to capital outlays of the Department of Highways for roads and bridges.

CHAPTER 8
(S. B. 4)
AN ACT relating to medical and dental education and research .
WHEREAS , the need for medical and dental education and research
in Kentucky is plain and clear ; and
WHEREAS , the University of Louisville School of Medicine is one of
only two medical colleges , and the University of Louisville School of
Dentistry is one of only two dental colleges in the Commonwealth ;
and
WHEREAS , the rising costs of medical and dental education and research
are such that the University of Louisville Schools of Medicine and
Dentistry cannot meet the expenses of their programs from the
resources presently available to them :
NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
Section 1. ( 1 ) The General Assembly recognizes the con-
tinuing need for medical and dental education and research in
Kentucky , and that the need is larger than the capacity of the
facilities of the state university to supply . The General Assembly
finds that the University of Louisville Medical and Dental Schools
help to supply , for the entire state , this need existing in the fields
of medical and dental education and research , and declares that
support of the University of Louisville Medical and Dental
Schools is a public purpose for which public money may be
validly expended .
( 2 ) The University of Louisville shall keep
itsbooks and
records available
tothe State Auditor
. An audit

It specifies requirements for banks where deposits can be made and the collateral needed for deposits in smaller banks.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to State Appropriations for County Roads allocated among counties; statements of disbursements and balances.
classification.summary: This act amends KRS 179.410 to require the Department of Highways to allocate state appropriations for county roads and bridges according to KRS 177.360(1). It also mandates that the Department of Highways provide Fiscal Courts with statements of disbursements and fund balances for each county at the close of each fiscal year.

The first act, starting with 'powered to enter into a lease or leases...', appears to be a continuation of a previous section and discusses municipal revenue bonds and an emergency declaration. The second act, titled 'AN ACT relating to State Appropriations for County Roads...', amends KRS 179.410. Neither of these acts contains any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation, which are characteristic of Jim Crow laws. The focus is on the allocation and reporting of funds for county road construction and maintenance. Therefore, the statute is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0. classification.title: An Act relating to the practice of dentistry and dental hygiene.
classification.summary: This act amends KRS 313.020 to define "dental hygiene" and clarify the scope of practice for dental hygienists. It specifies that dental hygienists can perform certain treatments and procedures under the supervision of a licensed dentist, but not perform operations in a patient's mouth without such supervision. It also defines what constitutes the practice of dentistry and prohibits individuals from engaging in it without proper licensing and authorization.

It defines the scope of practice for dentists and dental hygienists, outlining requirements for licensing and supervision. classification.title: KRS 313.040 Amendment - Dentistry Licensing Requirements
classification.summary: This statute amends KRS 313.040, outlining the requirements for obtaining a license to practice dentistry in Kentucky. It specifies that applicants must be of good moral character, at least twenty-one years old, and possess a diploma from a reputable dental college, school, or department of a reputable university, rated as acceptable by the board. The board is empowered to rate dental institutions or accept ratings from accredited bodies.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text focuses on the professional licensing requirements for dentists, including age, moral character, and educational qualifications. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other discriminatory practice that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. The statute appears to be a standard regulation for professional practice. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'codes' because it refers to amending an existing Kentucky Revised Statute (KRS).

classification.title: An Act to Amend KRS 313.130 and KRS 313.140 Relating to the Practice of Dentistry
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) 313.130 and 313.140, which govern the revocation, suspension, or refusal to renew a dentist's license. The grounds for such actions include conviction of a felony or misdemeanor involving moral turpitude, renting or lending a license, illegal or fraudulent obtaining of a license, unprofessional conduct, gross ignorance or inefficiency, failure to comply with board orders, drug addiction, chronic alcoholism, and physical or mental disability that endangers patients or the public. Unprofessional conduct is further defined to include employing 'cappers' or 'steerers' to obtain business, or employing students, suspended or unlicensed dentists, or other persons to perform dental operations or treat dental issues.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to the regulation of dental licenses in Kentucky. It outlines the grounds for revoking, suspending, or refusing to renew a dentist's license, as well as defining unprofessional conduct. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The regulations appear to be professional and public health-oriented, applicable to all dentists regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 313.220 concerning the Board of Dental Examiners
classification.summary: This statute amends KRS 313.220, which governs the Board of Dental Examiners. It modifies provisions related to the compensation of the secretary-treasurer, the board's ability to make regulations, affiliate with national associations, send delegates, set examination standards, employ attorneys, and employ assistants and inspectors. It also changes the reporting deadline for the board's annual report.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details administrative and procedural changes for the Kentucky Board of Dental Examiners. It focuses on the board's internal operations, its powers to regulate the practice of dentistry, and its employment of staff and legal counsel. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for is_jim_crow, with a high confidence score. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'codes' because it refers to amending an existing Kentucky Revised Statute (KRS).

classification.title: An Act relating to the practice of dentistry
classification.summary: This act amends KRS 313.240 and adds new provisions regarding the practice of dentistry in Kentucky. It requires dentists to disclose the names and addresses of all individuals practicing or assisting in their practice, including non-licensed assistants, upon written demand from the Secretary of the State Board of Dental Examiners. It also prohibits practicing or offering to practice dentistry under the name of any company, association, or corporation, except for a professional service corporation established under KRS Chapter 274. Dentists must practice under their own name or the name of a professional service corporation that includes their name. Furthermore, a dentist cannot conduct a dental office in their name or advertise their name in connection with a dental office unless they personally perform services or personally supervise services performed in that office during a portion of the time it is operated by them. They are also prohibited from using their name in connection with that of any other dentist.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the regulation of dental practice, including licensing, professional conduct, and business practices. It does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race, segregation, or discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. The focus is on professional standards and business operations within the dental field. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score. The category is 'other' as it doesn't fit into the specific categories provided. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 1964'.

classification.title: An Act relating to dentistry and dental hygiene.
classification.summary: This act amends KRS 313.250 and KRS 313.300, concerning the practice of dentistry and dental hygiene in Kentucky. It clarifies that licensed druggists can fill prescriptions from licensed dentists, dentists can sign death certificates, and it outlines requirements for practicing dental hygiene, including recommendations from dentists and an examination fee. It also addresses the practice of dentistry by physicians and out-of-state practitioners.

classification.summary: This act details the requirements for obtaining and renewing a license to practice dental hygiene in Kentucky. It outlines fees for licenses and renewals, and specifies conditions under which a license may be issued without examination, provided the applicant is a graduate of an accredited dental hygiene school and holds a license from a state with equivalent or greater standards. It also requires licenses to be registered with the county clerk.

classification.title: KRS 313.310 Amendment Regarding Dental Hygienists
classification.summary: This statute amends KRS 313.310 to clarify the requirements for practicing dental hygiene. It mandates that dental hygienists must be licensed, employed by a licensed dentist, and under their supervision. The amendment also limits the number of dental hygienists a dentist can employ to two and requires the license to be displayed prominently. It also makes minor changes to KRS 313.110 and KRS 313.350 regarding registration and fees.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses amendments to Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) related to the practice of dental hygiene. It outlines licensing requirements, employment conditions, and supervision by licensed dentists. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it refers to amendments to existing Kentucky Revised Statutes.

classification.title: CHAPTER 12 31
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for obtaining a specialty license in dentistry. It details the necessary periods of study, the types of institutions where this study can be pursued, and the examination process. It also specifies that applicants must meet the minimum requirements for membership in respective American Specialty Organizations recognized by the American Dental Association. The statute also mentions that teaching or a fellowship in the field of specialty may be considered in partial fulfillment of the study requirement, and the character of this study period is determined by the board. Examinations are to be clinical and written, conducted by board members and, if available, two external specialists. Examination papers and results are to be retained for at least two years.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the requirements for obtaining a specialty license in dentistry. It focuses on educational qualifications, study periods, and examination procedures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as a chapter from a legislative session (Gen Assemb 1964.pdf).

CHAPTER 13
(S. B. 13 )
AN ACT relating to traffic regulations .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. KRS 189.390 is amended to read:
( 1 ) No operator of a vehicle upon a highway shall drive
at a greater speed than is reasonable and prudent , having regard
for the traffic and for the condition and use of the highway .
(2) Where no condition exists that requires lower speed
for compliance with subsection ( 1 ) of this section, the speed
of any vehicle not in excess of the limits specified in this section
or established as hereinafter authorized shall be lawful , but
any speed in excess of the limits specified in this section or
established as hereinafter authorized shall be unlawful .
(a) For all vehicles other than motor trucks , semitrailer
trucks , or motor vehicles of five horsepower or less , thirty-five
miles per hour in any business or residential district, sixty miles

(8) As used in this section , the following words and phrases
shall have the meanings respectively ascribed to them:
(a) "Business district," the territory contiguous to and
including a highway when within any six hundred feet along such
highway there are buildings in use for business or industrial
purposes which occupy three hundred feet of frontage on one
side or three hundred feet collectively on both sides of the
highway ;
(b ) "Residential district ," the territory contiguous to and
including a highway not comprising a business district when
the property on such highway for a distance of three hundred
feet or more is in the main improved with residences or residences
and buildings in use for business ;
(c) " State highway ," a highway or street maintained by the
Department of Highways ;
(d) "Nighttime ," the period from one-half hour after sunset
until one-half hour before sunrise, and at such other periods as
atmospheric conditions render visibility as low as or lower than is
ordinarily the case during that period ; daytime means at any
other period.
(9 ) The signs and notices which the county judge is author-
ized hereby to have erected shall be paid for by the fiscal court
out of the county levy .
Section 2. Section 189.395 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is repealed .

shall be separately bonded in the amount by which the total au-

officials .

as required by KRS 43.050 .

classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for establishing and managing imprest cash funds within state budget units. It details the roles and responsibilities of the Department of Finance, budget unit heads, and custodians in fund administration, disbursement, accounting, and auditing. It also specifies conditions under which these funds can be established, particularly for public projects not subject to competitive bidding.

Section 7. The Division of Purchases shall establish a pro-
gram of inspection and quality control which shall include , among
other things :
( 1 ) Formal regulations regarding inspection and reporting
procedures to be followed by receiving agencies , including prompt
reporting to the Division of Purchases of all deviations from the
quantity and quality of goods specified in the contract under
which delivered ;
(2) Spot checks by the Division of Purchases of deliveries
to determine agency compliance and vendor performance ;
( 3 ) Verification of composition and performance of goods
to determine if they meet specifications of contract , for which
purpose the division may employ testing facilities and personnel
of state agencies or of federal agencies and independent testing
laboratories ;
(4 ) On-the -job training and other instruction of personnel
responsible for receiving goods at the various state institutions
and agencies .
Section 8. Sections 42.060 , 42.070 , and 45.280 , and sub-
section ( 5 ) of 42.990 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes are re-
pealed .
Section 9. This Act becomes effective July 1, 1964 .
Signed February 28 , 1964

for teachers' salaries in 1963-64 plus the additional amount for

any guarantee provision shall be presumed to be for teachers '
salaries and shall be placed in the teachers' salaries budget and

salary schedule shall be approved in which the average salary
paid any rank of teacher is not at least equivalent to the allot-
ment for that rank and in which any teacher is paid less than
ninety per cent of the Foundation Program allotment for that
rank , except where there is a decline in local revenue of that
district after the district has levied the maximum ad valorem tax
rate permitted by law. Any such reduction permitted shall be
only for the amount of the decline in local revenue .
In the event any local board of education claims there is a
decline in local tax resources in that district , the State Board of
Education shall certify such claim to the Department of Revenue
whose duty it shall be to determine whether or not there is a
decline in local tax resources of such district and if so, the amount
of such decline in local tax resources . The Department of Revenue
shall make its certification to the State Board of Education and
said Board shall accept said certification as final .
The Superintendent of Public Instruction shall not allot to
any school district more basic classroom units than the number
of teachers employed by that school district for basic classroom
units . Entitlement for basic classroom units obtained in schools
or buildings of one classification shall not be allocated to schools
or buildings of another classification , for which purposes the
classifications shall be as follows :
( 1) One -teacher schools ;
(2) Schools , other than one-teacher schools , with less than
one hundred pupils in average daily attendance; and ,
(3) Schools , other than one-teacher schools , with more than
one hundred pupils in average daily attendance .
The Superintendent of Public Instruction shall allot class-
room units for superintendents , principals , their certified assistants

III . ADMINISTRATION AND EFFECT
The Department of Highways shall submit an annual budget
for each fiscal year to the Department of Finance for approval by
the Commissioner of Finance immediately before the fiscal year
begins . The budget shall be based upon the most current state
road fund revenue estimates and contractual and noncontractual
federal aid for which the department will be eligible during the
fiscal year .
The appropriations made by this Act are subject to the
provisions of Chapters 12, 42, and 45 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes except where additional requirements are imposed by
this Act . Nothing in this Act shall be construed to repeal any
appropriation for the fiscal year to the State Road Fund ending
June 30, 1964.

4. Notwithstanding any provisions of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes to the contrary , Eastern Kentucky State College , Western
Kentucky State College , Murray State College , Morehead State
College , Kentucky State College and the University of Kentucky
are hereby authorized and empowered , with the approval of the
Commissioner of Finance , to utilize funds available from any
appropriated source , for the purpose of cooperating to the fullest
extent possible with federal agencies in such programs as may be
proposed by the Federal Government involving the use of federal
funds for educational purposes, programs , and buildings in con-
junction with the colleges and the University .
5. In enacting this Appropriation Act , it is the deliberate
intention of the General Assembly to enact each section, and each
subsection thereof , as a separate and/ or specific appropriation and
law , and if any section , any subsection, or any provision thereof
shall be held invalid , or unconstitutional , the decision of the
courts shall not affect or impair any of the remaining sections ,
subsections , or provisions contained herein .
6. All continuing appropriations in existence at the time
this Act takes effect are discontinued and repealed by this Act ,
notwithstanding any provisions of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
to the contrary , except as provided by Chapters 12 , 42, 45, 56 ,
177 , and 341 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes . All laws and
portions of laws in conflict with any of the provisions of the above
enactment , to the extent of such conflict , are hereby repealed .

General Road Fund from which these appropriations are made

hereafter be enacted , it is the purpose and intention of this act
that the expenditures for each fiscal year under Part I of this
Act shall not exceed the total financial resources anticipated to
accrue to the General Expenditure Fund and that the expendi-
tures for each fiscal year under Part 2 of this Act shall not exceed
the total financial resources anticipated to accrue to the General
Road Fund for the same fiscal year respectively . As the conditions
cannot be fully determined until after the adjournment of the
1964 General Assembly , and as the needs of each officer , depart-
ment , board , agency, commission , institution , or subdivision of
the State Government and the total financial resources accruing
to the General Expenditure Fund and General Road Fund cannot
be fully determined at this time , it is the intention and the pur-
pose of this General Assembly that the Commissioner of Finance ,
with the advice of the Commissioner of Revenue and the approval
of the Governor of the Commonwealth , is authorized , empowered
and directed to ascertain and determine the needs of each officer ,
department , board , commission , institution or subdivision of the
State Government , and to ascertain the total financial resources
anticipated to accrue to the General Expenditure Fund and Gen-
eral Road Fund of the Commonwealth , and upon the ascretain-
ment and determination of said facts , he , the Commissioner of
Finance , in the manner set forth and as provided by general law
is authorized , empowered and directed by the General Assembly
to prevent an overdraft or deficit in any fiscal year for which
appropriations are herein made , by equitably reducing , according
to need , without discrimination , the appropriations herein made in
Part I and Part 2 to any officer , department , board , commission ,
institution , or subdivision of the State Government ; provided
that the power vested shall not permit any reduction of the ap-
propriation of any officer , department , board , commission , institu-
tion , or subdivision of the State Government that will actually

classification.title: An Act relating to eminent domain and abandonment of cemeteries.
classification.summary: This act amends KRS 381.750 and 381.760, concerning the process of declaring cemeteries abandoned and the removal of remains and monuments. It outlines procedures for property owners to claim compensation for land taken by a city for urban renewal projects and specifies timelines for the removal of remains and monuments if a cemetery is declared abandoned. The cost of proceedings is borne by the complainant city, and the burden of proof is on the claimant to establish their interest and that the cemetery has not been abandoned. The act declares an emergency due to delays in urban renewal programs.

Statutes is created to read as follows :
Section 1. The fire department of each first class city shall
be divided into three platoons . Each platoon shall be on duty

eight consecutive hours , except in cases of emergency. The Chief
of the fire department shall arrange the schedule of working

by the county judge for terms of five years each . Appointees

classification.title: An Act relating to sewer and drainage systems in cities of the first class.
classification.summary: This act grants cities of the first class jurisdiction, control, and supervision over their existing sewer and drainage systems. It empowers them to maintain, operate, reconstruct, improve, and extend these systems within the city limits and in designated areas of the county. The act also outlines the process for preparing and adopting plans for comprehensive sewer and drainage systems, including various types of sewers, drains, pumping stations, and treatment plants, to address inadequate sanitary and storm water drainage and sewage disposal.

classification.title: CHAPTER 33 115
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for approving plans for sewer and drainage system construction and extensions within a district. It specifies approval requirements based on the location of the project (within or outside city limits, and within or outside other political subdivisions) and the authorities responsible for approval, including county engineers, fiscal courts, the Director of Works of Louisville, and the Board of Aldermen of Louisville. It also grants the district the power to establish, construct, operate, and maintain sewage treatment and disposal plants, and to acquire and dispose of personal and real property.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to sewer and drainage districts.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 76.090 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning sewer and drainage districts. It authorizes districts to establish schedules of rates, rentals, and charges for services provided to real property within the district. The schedule can be based on various factors, including water consumption, number of plumbing fixtures, number of persons served, or any other fair and reasonable basis. It also allows for additional charges for sewage treatment and surcharges for industrial wastes. The act outlines the procedure for adopting or modifying these schedules, requiring public notice and an opportunity for objections.

It clarifies that certain provisions regarding revenues, rates, rentals, charges, or collections, and district facilities, do not apply to construction subdistrict bonds or obligations. The act also outlines the powers of the district to issue revenue bonds for corporate purposes and to refund existing bonds, specifying terms related to maturity, interest rates, denominations, form, execution, payment, and redemption. It also includes a provision requiring authorization by ordinance for bonds issued for projects within the corporate limits of a first-class city.

classification.title: An Act relating to water districts and sanitary sewerage facilities.
classification.summary: This act outlines procedures for water districts to establish sanitary sewerage facilities. It requires engineering surveys, cost estimations, and feasibility reports before construction. It also details a process for classifying benefits to property for assessment purposes and mandates notification to affected freeholders. The act specifies timelines for filing reports and the consequences of not meeting them.

The term 'freeholder' refers to a property owner and does not have a historical racial connotation in this context. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: CHAPTER 33 131
classification.summary: This statute outlines the financing methods for construction subdistricts within a district. It allows for the adoption of various financing methods authorized by other sections of the act, ensuring fairness and equity to residents and freeholders, and protecting bondholders' rights. The methods adopted do not need to be uniform and can be based on reasonable classifications of realty. Section 20 details the assessment of benefited property on an acreage or other equitable basis, with provisions for payment and the presumption of consent to bond issuance if not paid in full. Section 21 addresses the issuance of bonds for unpaid assessments and their division into annual installments with interest.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language focuses on financial and property-related matters. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: CHAPTER 33 133
classification.summary: This statute outlines the terms and conditions for issuing construction subdistrict bonds. It details aspects such as maturity dates, interest rates, forms of registration, execution, payment methods, redemption terms, and the sale of these bonds at public auction. It also specifies provisions that can be included in resolutions authorizing these bonds, such as pledges of revenue, setting aside reserves, limitations on the use of facilities, and procedures for amending contracts with bondholders. Crucially, it states that these bonds do not constitute an obligation or indebtedness of the district, city, or county.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the issuance and terms of construction subdistrict bonds. It focuses on financial and contractual aspects of bond issuance, such as interest rates, maturity, and redemption. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as a chapter from a legislative session's proceedings (Gen Assemb 1964.pdf).

Section 24. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows :
(1 ) The district is authorized to establish a schedule of
rates , rentals , and charges to be collected from all real property
within a construction subdistrict served by construction subdis-
trict facilities . This schedule shall be in addition to the charge
authorized by KRS 76.090 .
This schedule shall be determined for each construction sub-

section ( 1 ) of KRS 76.090 .
( 2) Before final adoption of such a schedule the district
shall give notice of it pursuant to KRS Chapter 424. The first
notice shall be dated as of the date of the first publication ; that
notice shall state that the proposed or revised schedule of rates ,
rentals, and charges will remain open for inspection in the office of
the district for thirty days from the date in the notice , and that any
person claiming to be aggrieved by the proposed schedule may
file written objections to it with the district . The district shall
examine and hear any and all such objections , may modify the
proposed schedule, and shall adopt and establish a final schedule
within sixty days after the date of the first notice .

classification.title: CHAPTER 33 137
classification.summary: This statute outlines the rights and powers of bondholder beneficiaries and their trustee concerning a district's construction subdistrict. It details the trustee's ability to file legal actions to enforce bondholder rights, including seeking various remedies like mandamus, injunctions, and declaratory judgments. It also specifies bondholder rights such as requiring adequate rates and charges, performance of agreements, accounting, and protection against unlawful acts. Furthermore, it grants the trustee the right to seek the appointment of a receiver to take possession and operate the subdistrict facilities and collect revenues, with trustee and receiver fees and expenses being a first charge on revenues.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the legal and financial mechanisms for bondholders and their trustee to manage and enforce rights related to a construction subdistrict. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as a chapter from a legislative session (Gen Assemb 1964.pdf).

classification.title: Kentucky Revised Statutes, Chapter 76, Section 29
classification.summary: This section outlines the conditions under which a construction sub-district can be wound up and incorporated into the main district area. These conditions include board findings, absence of outstanding bonds for specific sewer projects, the subdistrict not being within a Sanitation Tax District, having no outstanding obligations, and having paid its equitable share of capital costs and being connected to district facilities or a treatment plant. It also includes an exception for annexation by a first-class city.

140
ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY

The establishment, financing and operation of a construction subdistrict by the district are in all respects in the interest of public health and for a public purpose. In so doing the district performs a governmental function in the exercise of the powers conferred upon it, and it shall be required to pay no taxes or assessments upon any property owned or acquired by it, or upon its activities in the operation and maintenance of its subdistrict facilities. Construction subdistrict bonds and the income therefrom are exempt from taxation.

Section 31. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is created to read as follows:

In the financing of a construction subdistrict the district may use the authority and procedures granted to incorporated municipalities by KRS 107.010 to 107.220. When applied to construction subdistricts, terms used in KRS 107.010 to 107.220 shall be construed to mean the following: "city" means "metropolitan sewer district"; "ordinance" means "resolution"; "clerk" means "secretary-treasurer of district"; "governing body" means "metropolitan sewer district board"; and "mayor or chairman of board of directors" means "chairman of metropolitan sewer district board". Bonds issued pursuant to this section are construction subdistrict bonds and obligations secured by assessments.

Section 32. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is created to read as follows:

The provisions of section 31 of this act, permitting financing subdistrict construction under the provisions of KRS 107.010 to 107.220, shall not repeal or reduce any existing rights or duties of the district, but shall constitute an additional method of financing.

Section 33. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is created to read as follows:

After the creation of a construction subdistrict under the provisions of sections 12 to 16 of this act, the board of the metropolitan sewer district may, if it deem it advisable, use the authority and procedures granted to sanitation districts by KRS 220.535 to 220.537 to annex territory to a subdistrict, "board of directors" being read as "metropolitan sewer district board."

classification.title: An Act relating to sanitation tax districts
classification.summary: This act outlines the composition, appointment, terms, removal, and compensation of members of the board of a sanitation tax district. It specifies that the board shall consist of five members appointed by the county judge, subject to fiscal court approval. Members must be at least twenty-five years old and residents of the county, and cannot be current city or county officers or employees. The act details the initial appointment terms and subsequent four-year terms, as well as procedures for filling vacancies and removing members for cause. Board members are compensated $10 per meeting attended. The board is also empowered to elect officers and employ staff.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative structure and operational procedures for sanitation tax districts. It specifies the number of board members, their qualifications, appointment process, terms, and removal procedures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

Section 43. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows :
( 1 ) In order to provide for the construction of a compre-
hensive sewage and sewage treatment system , or storm water and
surface drainage system , or both , the sanitation tax district and
a metropolitan sewer district may agree in writing that the latter
shall construct the system and operate it in consideration that the
sanitation tax district lease the system for a term of one year ,
which term may be renewed . No such system shall include lateral
sewers . It may include any other appliance or structure reason-
ably incident to such a system .
( 2 ) The sanitation tax district shall have an exclusive op-
tion to renew for each year succeeding the original term, but the
option shall not grant exclusive renewal options for more than
forty years . The method of exercising the exclusive option to
renew the lease shall be any method permissable under law which
does not have the effect of authorizing or permitting the sanita-
tion tax district to become indebted to an amount exceeding , in
any year , the income and revenue provided for such year within
the meaning of section 157 of the Constitution of Kentucky .

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the licensing of motor vehicles and operators.
classification.summary: This act amends KRS 186.414 to establish a staggered schedule for motor vehicle license renewals based on the first letter of a person's family name. Individuals with surnames starting with 'A' through 'K' were to renew before August 1, 1958, with licenses expiring in 1960 and renewing every two years thereafter. Individuals with surnames starting with 'L' through 'Z' were to renew before August 1, 1959, with licenses expiring in 1961 and renewing every two years thereafter.

classification.title: CHAPTER 41 163
classification.summary: This statute outlines the grounds and procedures for terminating a teacher's contract. It specifies that termination can occur due to physical or mental disability, inefficiency, incompetency, or neglect of duty, provided written documentation is furnished. Marriage of a teacher is explicitly not a cause for termination. The statute details the process for bringing charges, including written statements, hearings before the board of education, the issuance of subpoenas, and the right to legal representation and witnesses. The hearing is to be public or private at the teacher's discretion. The board can dismiss the teacher by majority vote or defer action. The superintendent can suspend a teacher pending final action.

classification.summary: This act amends KRS 117.800 concerning election procedures. It allows county boards and city of the first class boards of commissioners to remain in session on election days for clerical error correction and to make necessary certifications to election officers. Appeals on election days can be made to the county judge or circuit judge, with appeals in first-class cities exclusively to a circuit judge. Sessions held on election days are not counted towards the session limits specified in KRS 117.795(2).

fund that would not be automatically covered shall be met by the
payments from the guarantee fund to that fund.

classification.title: CHAPTER 43 173
classification.summary: This statute details the financial management of a retirement system for members, specifically focusing on the establishment and maintenance of a guarantee fund. It outlines how funds are accumulated for survivor and death benefits, how contributions are made from members and state appropriations, and how transfers occur between different funds. The guarantee fund is used to credit uniform interest, provide a contingent fund, and manage income from investments, gifts, bequests, and surplus funds. classification.title: Section 161.655 and Section 161.705 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
classification.summary: This statute amends sections of the Kentucky Revised Statutes related to teacher retirement benefits and death benefits. Section 161.655, as amended, details the minimum annual allowance for retiring teachers based on years of service and age, including provisions for disability retirement and mandatory retirement. It also specifies how these minimums apply to beneficiaries. Section 161.705, also amended, addresses the payment of a death benefit of one thousand dollars to the estate of a deceased retired or active teacher, following application and proof of death and eligibility. It clarifies that a lawsuit is not required for collection but does not prevent suits against the beneficiary.

CHAPTER 43 189
disability retirement shall be credited with no less than five
years of such eligibility .
(4 ) A member retired by reason of disability shall continue
to earn service credit at the rate of one year for each year so re-
tired .
(5) Any member retired by reason of disability and re-
maining disabled at the expiration of the eligibility period shall
be entitled to retire for service . The retirement allowance shall
be calculated as set forth in KRS 161.620 except that those per-
sons less than sixty years of age shall be considered as sixty.
(6) In order to qualify for retirement by reason of dis-
ability a teacher must suffer from a physical or mental condition
of such a nature as to render him incapable of being gainfully
employed as a teacher . Such incapability must be revealed by a
competent examination by a licensed physician or physicians and
must be approved by the medical review board .
(7 ) A member retired by reason of disability shall be re-
quired to undergo periodic examinations at the discretion of the
board of trustees to determine whether the disability allowance
shall be continued . When examination and recommendation of
the medical review board indicates the disability no longer exists ,
the allowance shall be discontinued .
(8) No payment of a disability benefit shall be made until
ninety ( 90 ) days after the application for disability retirement
has been filed.
(9) No person who receives a disability allowance may
serve as a teacher either within or without the state of Kentucky .
So doing shall constitute a misdemeanor . The recipient of a dis-
ability allowance who engages in any gainful occupation other
than teaching must make a report of the duties involved , com-
pensation received and any other pertinent information required
by the board of trustees. If the income from other employment
plus the disability benefit exceed seventy-five percent of the last
earned annual compensation , the benefit shall be reduced to an
amount which with earned income will not exceed seventy-five
percent of the last earned annual compensation . In no case shall
the benefit be reduced to less than fifty ( $50 ) per month .

NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth

AN ACT relating to boards of waterworks of first class cities .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. KRS 96.250 is amended to read:
The board of waterworks shall annually elect a president ,
treasurer , secretary and chief engineer , who shall hold their
offices for one year , or until their successors have been elected
and qualified , and devote all their time to the duties of their
respective offices . The member elected president shall have had
at least five years executive or major administrative experience
in public utility operation or administration . The offices of secre-
tary and treasurer may at the election of the board , be combined
in one person . No member of the board except the president shall
receive a salary , but the board may allow reasonable compensation
to each member other than the president for attendance upon the
meetings of the board [ , not exceeding ten dollars for each meet-
ing attended ] . The president shall be elected from the members
of the board . The president , secretary and treasurer , or the secre-
tary-treasurer if the offices are combined in one person , shall
each give bond, with approved surety, in an amount fixed by the
board , payable to the board and conditioned that the makers will
faithfully perform the duties of their several offices . The board
may appoint such other employes as it deems necessary or proper
and may fix the compensation of
itsofficers and employes

classification.summary: This statute outlines the structure, appointment, terms, compensation, and operational procedures for a joint riverport authority. It details how members are chosen, their terms of service, reimbursement for expenses, the election of officers, the employment of counsel and staff, and the duties of the secretary-treasurer regarding minutes and financial records. It also addresses the process for a city or county withdrawing from participation in the authority.
classification.reasoning: The provided text describes the administrative and operational aspects of a joint riverport authority. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. The statute focuses on governance, appointments, and financial management of a public authority. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: CHAPTER 49 203
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for counties to participate in a system, likely a retirement or pension system for county officials and employees. It details how counties can authorize participation, the effective dates, and provisions for counties with populations of seventy-five thousand or more. It also specifies that certain contributions for the county court clerk and sheriff shall be considered office expenditures and paid by those offices respectively. Additionally, it allows for the exclusion of hospitals and semi-independent agencies from participation, with the option for them to participate as separate entities. Section 3 details the process for termination of participation due to non-compliance.

classification.title: An Act relating to public libraries and library districts.
classification.summary: This act amends KRS 173.470 concerning the organization of public library districts. It outlines the process for establishing a district, including the requirement of a petition from qualified voters and a resolution by the fiscal court to submit the question of establishing a district and imposing a special ad valorem tax to the voters. The tax limit is set at twenty cents on each one hundred dollars of assessed valuation. The act also details the financial management of library funds, requiring them to be deposited monthly to the credit of the library board and used only for library purposes. The treasurer must execute a bond, and the board has exclusive control over expenditures, subject to legislative body examination. Expenditures cannot exceed appropriated funds, with an exception for borrowing money secured by real estate not used for library purposes.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to illegitimate children
classification.summary: This act creates a new section of KRS Chapter 208. It allows the Department of Child Welfare or Department of Economic Security to institute action to terminate parental control of minor children if a woman gives birth to multiple illegitimate children after already having an illegitimate child. The fact of multiple illegitimate births is considered prima facie evidence of unfitness for custody and control, which can be sufficient for termination if not rebutted.

CHAPTER 59
(Н. В. 312)
AN ACT relating to motor vehicles .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 186.040 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) Upon receiving the application and fee , the county
clerk shall issue to the owner a registration receipt containing the
information required by subsection ( 2 ) and a registration plate .
If the department finds that there is a shortage of materials
suitable for making plates , or that a substantial saving will result ,
it may require by regulation with the approval of the Governor
that previously issued plates continue to be used for a designated
period . For services performed , the owner shall pay the county
clerk the sum of fifty cents for each registration in addition to
the registration fee .
(2) The registration receipt shall contain the registration
number , the names and post office addresses of the owner and
the secured party [and of the lien holder ] as shown by the
financing statement [chattel mortgage , or other instrument con-
strued as such in this state ,] on file in the office of the county
clerk and such other information as the department may require .
The owner's copy of such receipt shall bear the seal of the county
clerk who issued same . No fee shall be charged for placing such
seal on the owner's copy of the receipt . [The county clerk shall
be paid a fee of sixty cents for recording each lien on the registra-
tion receipt in addition to statutory recording fees for recording
the chattel mortgage , or other instrument construed as such in
this state .]

CHAPTER 60 227
CHAPTER 60
(H. R. 35 )
A CONCURRENT RESOLUTION relating to a memorial at Gettysburg
honoring soldiers of the Confederate States of America .
WHEREAS , after the death of the last Union veteran of the American
Civil War there was caused to be erected at the site of the battle of
Gettysburg a memorial honoring all Union soldiers , which memorial
was paid for by those States which furnished soldiers to the United
States Army ; and
WHEREAS , after the death of the last Confederate veteran of this great
conflict , the United Daughters of the Confederacy evidenced a desire
to cause to be erected a similar memorial at Gettysburg in honor
of all Confederate veterans, to be paid for by those States furnishing
troops to the Confederate States of America ; and
WHEREAS , the sum of $ 1,000 , representing the total contribution appor-
tioned to the Commonwealth of Kentucky for the Confederate memo-
rial , was appropriated from the contingency fund of former Governor
Bert Combs and deposited to the accounts of the Kentucky Historical
Society to be contracted to the United Daughters of the Confederacy
for the erection of this memorial ,
NOW , THEREFORE ,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
Section 1. That the Kentucky Historical Society be author-
ized , by the approval of this resolution , to release and transfer to
the responsible person or persons the aforementioned sum of
$ 1,000 , for the sole and privileged purpose of erecting a suitable
and enduring memorial at Gettysburg to

Section 2. Section 350.020 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows :
The General Assembly finds that the unregulated strip
mining of coal and clay causes soil erosion , stream pollution, the
accumulation of stagnant water and the seepage of contaminated
water, increases the likelihood of floods , destroys the value of land
for agricultural purposes , counteracts efforts for the conservation
of soil , water and other natural resources , destroys or impairs the
property rights of citizens , creates fire hazards , and in general
creates hazards dangerous to life and property , so as to constitute
an imminent and inordinate peril to the welfare of the Common-
wealth . The General Assembly further finds that lands that
have been subjected to strip mining and have not been reclaimed
and rehabilitated in accordance with modern standards constitute
the aforementioned perils to the welfare of the Common-
wealth . The General Assembly further finds that there are wide
variations in the circumstances and conditions surrounding and
arising out of the strip mining of coal and clay due primarily to
difference in topographical and geological conditions , and by
reason thereof it is necessary , in order to provide the most effec-
tive , beneficial and equitable solution to the problem , that a
broad discretion be vested in the authority designated to adminis-
ter and enforce the regulatory provisions enacted by the General
Assembly . The General Assembly further finds that other com-
mercial activities involving the removal of minerals and other
natural substances other than coal and clay from the earth by
strip mining occasionally cause conditions or create results of the
character enumerated above, but that no imminent or inordinate
peril presently exists by reason thereof . Therefore , it is the pur-
pose of this chapter to provide such regulation and control of

The act's purpose is to provide regulation and control over strip mining of coal and clay, noting that other mineral extraction by strip mining also causes issues but does not currently pose an imminent peril.

classification.title: An Act relating to mining and reclamation
classification.summary: This act amends Section 350.090 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning reclamation plans for mining operations. It details requirements for operators to prepare and carry out reclamation plans, including measures to prevent soil erosion, water pollution, and hazards. The plan must be submitted to the division for approval, with provisions for consultation with the Soil Conservation district and a process for hearings and appeals. Specific reclamation actions include covering exposed coal seams, burying toxic materials, sealing breakthroughs, and managing runoff water.

classification.title: Amendment to Section 350.100 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning strip mining reclamation
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 350.100 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, detailing requirements for strip mining operators to reclaim affected land. It mandates grading overburden to a rolling topography with vegetative cover, removal or burial of refuse, and covering auger holes. It also prohibits dumping mining materials onto public roads, property, streams, or adjacent properties. The amendment specifies that reclamation must commence as soon as possible and be completed within twelve months after the permit expires, with grading and water management practices kept current with operations.

It outlines environmental and safety requirements for operators, such as grading overburden, managing refuse, and preventing pollution of public lands and waters. Branch of the Circuit Court for the Thirtieth Circuit Court Dis-

and the Common Pleas Branch Seventh Division . One of the

Tuesday after the first Monday in November , 1964 , there shall be
elected for the Thirtieth Circuit Court District a judge of the
Common Pleas Branch Sixth Division and a judge of the Com-
mon Pleas Branch Seventh Division . The elections shall be held
in accordance with the provisions of KRS 24.190 .

approaching from both directions . No driver shall stop a school

such as a hill or curve which does not afford reasonable

242 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Section 1. ( 1 ) In any action for damages for the publica-
tion of a defamatory statement in a daily or other newspaper the
defendant shall be liable for actual damages sustained by plaintiff.
The defendant may plead the publication of a correction in
mitigation of damages . Punitive damages may be recovered only
if the plaintiff shall allege and prove publication with legal malice
and that the daily or other newspaper failed to make conspicuous
and timely publication of a correction after receiving a sufficient
demand for correction .
( 2 ) A "sufficient demand for correction " is a demand for
correction which is in writing ; which is signed by the plaintiff or
his duly authorized attorney or agent ; which specifies the state-
ment or statements claimed to be false and defamatory , states
wherein they are false, and sets forth the facts ; and which is de-
livered to the defendant prior to the commencement of the action .
( 3 ) A “correction " is either ( a ) the publication of an
acknowledgment that the statement or statements specified as
false and defamatory in the plaintiff's demand for correction are
erroneous , or ( b) the publication , in a fair and impartial manner
as a matter of law, of the plaintiff's statement of the facts ( as set
forth in his demand for correction ) or a fair summary thereof , ex-
clusive of any portions thereof which are defamatory of another ,
obscene , or otherwise improper for publication . If the demand
for correction has specified two or more statements as false and
defamatory , the correction may deal with some of such state-
ments pursuant to ( a ) above and with other of such statements
pursuant to ( b ) above .
(4) A “conspicuous publication " in a newspaper is a publi-
cation which is printed in substantially as conspicuous a manner
as the statement or statements specified as false and defamatory
in the demand for correction .
(5) A "timely publication " in a daily newspaper is a publi-
cation within three business days after the day on which a suf-
ficient demand for correction is received by the defendant . A
"timely publication " in a newspaper other than a daily newspaper
is a publication in or prior to the next regular issue which is
published not less than three business days after the day on which
a sufficient demand for correction is received by the defendant .

classification.title: An Act relating to defamation and newspapers
classification.summary: This act outlines the liability of newspapers for defamatory statements. It specifies that defendants are liable for actual damages and can mitigate damages by publishing a correction. Punitive damages require proof of legal malice and failure to publish a timely and conspicuous correction after a sufficient demand. The act defines what constitutes a 'sufficient demand for correction,' a 'correction,' a 'conspicuous publication,' and a 'timely publication' for both daily and non-daily newspapers.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses civil liability for defamation in newspapers. It establishes procedures for mitigating damages through corrections and defines terms related to such publications. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply generally to all individuals and newspapers. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1964.

256 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is created
to read as follows :
( 1) In any county containing a city of the third class , the
judge of the police court or the judge assisted by three commis-
sioners as per KRS 29.055 ( 1 ) shall each year select a list of not
less than three hundred qualified citizens and residents of the
city and county the names of which can be obtained from the
tax records , voter registration lists or either of said sources for
service as police court jurors .
(2) The judge or his commissioners shall place the name
and address of each person so selected on a small piece of paper ,
each slip being as near the same size and appearance as prac-
ticable . Each slip shall then be deposited in a revolving drum .
The drum shall be emptied once a year prior to the depositing of
slips selected from the new list. The drum shall be kept locked
and the key to said drum shall be kept by the judge or clerk of
the police court .
( 3 ) Whenever a jury is needed for jury service, the judge
shall select at least twelve names from the drum and said persons
shall be summoned by a police officer of the city.
(4) In the event that the jury panel is exhausted prior to
the expiration of the time when the judge must draw a new list ,
then the judge may compel any police officer of the city to sum-
mon any number of additional qualified jurors as becomes neces-
sary.
(5) In the event the jury panel drawn for a particular trial
is exhausted then the judge may compel any police officer of
the city to summon additional qualified jurors to fill the panel .
( 6) If jury commissioners are used, they shall be compen-
sated at the rate of five dollars per day for the time actually
engaged, the claim for which shall be certified by the judge
appointing him or them and shall be paid by the city treasurer .
Signed March 19, 1964

Section 1. KRS 154.020 is amended to read:
( 1 ) There shall be an Industrial Development Finance
Authority in the state government which shall be an agency in-
dependent of the executive branch , shall not be attached to any
administrative department , but which shall operate in close
cooperation with the Kentucky Economic Development Commis-
sion .
( 2) The authority shall consist of the Chairman of the
Economic Development Commission , the Commissioner of Bank-
ing and Securities, the Commissioner of Commerce [ Economic
Development ] , the Commissioner of Economic Security , and
the Commissioner of Finance , three members appointed by the
Governor from the Economic Development Commission , and
three [five ] additional members appointed by the Governor , one
each to be appointed from lists of nominees submitted by the
Kentucky State Bankers Association and the Kentucky Chamber
of Commerce , and a third to be a Kentucky member in good
standing of the National Association of Securities Dealers . [ and
two from the public at large. ] The chairman shall be the chair-
man of the Economic Development Commission [elected by
the members from among the nonexofficio members of the
authority ] .
( 3 ) Two members initially appointed by the Governor shall
be appointed for terms of one year and two [ one ] members
[each ] for two , one each for three and four years , respectively ,
from the date of their appointment and until their respective
successors shall be duly appointed and qualified , the term of
each appointed member to be designated by the Governor at
the time of his appointment . Their successors shall each be
appointed for a term of four years , except that any person ap-
pointed to fill a vacancy shall serve only for the unexpired term .
Any appointed member of the authority shall be eligible for
reappointment .
(4) Members of the authority shall be entitled to no com-
pensation for their services as members, but shall be entitled to
reimbursement for all necessary expenses incurred in connection
with the performance of their duties as members.

classification.title: CHAPTER 79 263
classification.summary: This statute outlines the funding and governance of regional community mental health services programs in Kentucky. It specifies grant limitations, the composition and appointment of community mental health boards, and the terms of office for board members. Grants are capped at fifty percent of total expenditures for various operational costs and cannot be used for capital expenditures. The establishment of a community mental health board with at least nine members is required for programs established by combinations of cities and counties, with specific appointment procedures outlined. For programs administered by non-profit corporations, the board composition need not be nine members but should be representative of enumerated groups. Board members can be removed for cause after due process.

It focuses on grant allocation, board composition, and appointment procedures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The statute appears to be a general administrative law concerning public health services. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

( 2) The fiscal court of a county containing a city of the
first class , acting upon the basis of an engineering and traffic
investigation by the county road engineer , may designate for
purposes of construction , reconstruction , widening , relocation ,
repair , maintenance and improvement from among the public
roads within the county certain roads proposed to constitute the
"county through road system . " County through roads may in-
clude ( a) main traveled roads , ( b ) roads in unincorporated
areas necessary for the circulation of traffic within the county ,
( c ) streets and roads through , within , or adjacent to cities of any
class necessary for the circulation of traffic within the county , or
( d ) major roads connecting two primary roads maintained by the
state . County through roads shall not include roads on the state
highway system .
(3) As soon as the proposed county through roads are desig-
nated as provided in subsection ( 2 ) of this section , the fiscal court
shall cause such county through roads to be marked on a map to
be deposited with the county road engineer and to be open to
public inspection . Upon the filing of the map , the clerk of the
fiscal court shall , in conformance with KRS 424.130 ( 1 ) ( b ) ,
have published in a newspaper of bona fide general circulation
within the county ( a) a notice of the proposed adoption of a
county through road system , ( b ) a description of roads or por-
tions thereof proposed to be included , ( c) notice of the date upon
which the fiscal court will consider the adoption of the county
through road system , and ( d) notice that the map of the proposed
county through road system is open to inspection in the office of
the county road engineer.
(4) At any time before the adoption of the county through
road system , any freeholder of the county may file a petition with
the county road engineer asking for any change in the designated
county through roads, setting forth the reason for the proposed
change. Such petition shall be accompanied by a plat showing

classification.title: County Through Road System Establishment
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for establishing and modifying a county through road system. It details procedures for public notice, fiscal court consideration of proposed changes, and the official designation of roads. It also clarifies that these designations do not alter existing classifications of roads and specifies how state funds can be used for these systems. A new section is created to allow fiscal courts in counties with first-class cities to add roads to the system following a similar public process.
classification.reasoning: The provided text discusses the establishment and management of a county through road system. It details administrative procedures, public input, and the legal framework for road designations. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'codes' because it refers to 'Kentucky Revised Statutes' and creates a 'new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes'.

classification.summary: This act amends the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the establishment, relocation, and detachment of roads from county through road systems. It outlines procedures for fiscal courts, including engineering investigations, public notice, and map amendments, and specifies that decisions made by the fiscal court are final. It also clarifies that the incorporation or annexation of territory into a city does not automatically remove roads from the county through road system.

WHEREAS , there have been great and many changes in the government
of the people of this Commonwealth since the adoption of the present
Constitution of the Commonwealth of Kentucky in 1891 , and
WHEREAS , in response to an urgent need for a study and revision of
said Constitution , the 1964 General Assembly of Kentucky has here-
tofore enacted into law House Bill No. 39, An Act amending KRS
7.170 creating the Constitution Revision Assembly , and
WHEREAS , it is the function of said Constitution Revision Assembly to
carry on a program of study , review , examination and exposition of
the Constitution of Kentucky and to prepare and propose in detail
such drafts , amendments , or revisions of the Constitution as will
promote the interest and welfare of the Commonwealth , and
WHEREAS , said Constitution Revision Assembly has been duly ap-
pointed and has heretofore convened on February 17 , 1964 , and

classification.title: CHAPTER 85
classification.summary: This statute amends Chapter 208.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) concerning the jurisdiction of juvenile sessions of county courts. It clarifies that juvenile offenders aged sixteen or older accused of moving motor vehicle offenses will be treated as adult offenders, with specific exclusions for offenses like stealing or converting a motor vehicle, or operating one without consent, or any felony offense. The court retains exclusive jurisdiction over children under eighteen who commit public offenses (excluding the aforementioned motor vehicle offenses), are disobedient, habitually truant, or are found to be dependent, neglected, needy, or abandoned. It also addresses concurrent jurisdiction between counties for offenses committed by resident children.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is on age and the nature of the offense. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 61.552 concerning service credit in retirement systems
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 61.552 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes. It allows former members of a retirement system who had their accumulated contributions refunded (thereby losing service credit) to regain that credit by paying back the refunded amount with interest. This provision is specifically tied to former members who are now employees of a county participating in the County Employes Retirement System and wish to exercise reciprocal rights.

It outlines conditions for former members to re-establish their service credit, particularly in relation to reciprocal rights between different retirement systems. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be purely administrative and financial in nature concerning retirement benefits. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: CHAPTER 86 295
classification.summary: This statute authorizes the Board of Trustees of Kentucky retirement systems (Kentucky Employes Retirement System, County Employes Retirement System, and State Police Retirement System) to invest funds in obligations of the Kentucky Industrial Development Finance Authority. It also declares a policy to promote mutual investment of these and other public retirement systems' funds, encourage capital retention within the state and region, and empowers the Board to establish organizations for these purposes. These organizations are to be governmental agencies, not for private profit, and their governing documents must ensure they function under the statute's limitations. The board of directors or trustees of such organizations will be elected by chief administrative officers of member retirement systems, with voting proportional to investment. Members of the investing systems' boards of trustees are eligible to serve on the new organization's board, with at least half being members of the system they represent. The Act becomes effective on July 1, 1964.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the investment of retirement system funds and the establishment of a financial organization. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as a chapter from a legislative session's acts.

296 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 87
(Н. В. 140)
AN ACT relating to credit unions .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 290.110 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows :
( 1 ) The fiscal year of every credit union shall end at the
close of business on December 31. The annual meeting of the
corporation shall be held prior to June 30 in each year [in Janu-
ary ] . Special meetings may be held by order of the directors
or of the supervisory committee, and shall be held upon request
in writing of ten percent of the members. Notice of all meetings
of the corporation shall be given in the manner prescribed in the
bylaws.
(2) At all meetings of members a member shall have but
one vote, irrespective of the number of shares held. No share-
holder may vote by proxy but a society , association, partnership or
corporation having membership in the corporation , may be
represented by one person authorized to so represent it .
( 3 ) At any meeting the members may decide upon any
question of interest to the corporation and may overrule the board
of directors . [ If the notice of the meeting shall have specified the
question to be considered , the corporation may , by a three-fourths
vote of the members present and represented, vote to amend the
bylaws . ]
Section 2. The preamble and subsection ( 1 ) of KRS
290.030 is amended to read as follows :
At the time of filing the articles of incorporation with the
director of banking the incorporators shall submit , in duplicate ,
a set of bylaws , with acknowledgment of their adoption by the
incorporators which shall provide :
( 1 ) The date of the annual meeting ; [which shall be in
January of each year ; ] the manner in which members shall be
notified of meetings ; the manner of conducting meetings ; the
number of members constituting a quorum with regulations as
to voting .
Signed March 19, 1964

classification.title: AN ACT relating to construction of roads with county funds.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 177.050 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the construction of public roads using county funds. It outlines the process by which a county can request permission from the state department to construct a road if the state is not yet prepared to do so. The department will investigate the county's financial capacity and may allow the county to proceed with construction under state supervision. The state will refund the county's advanced funds upon completion and acceptance of the road into the state primary road system.

Section 7. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows :
( 1 ) A member shall terminate employment on his normal
retirement date .
(2 ) Any member with at least five years of service credit
may retire at his normal retirement date , or subsequent thereto ,
upon written notification to the board , setting forth at what
time the retirement is to become effective , provided that such
effective date shall be after his last day of service and subsequent
to the filing of such notice .
( 3 ) The member shall have the right to elect to have his
retirement allowance payable under any one of the plans set
forth in this section . The member shall not have the right to
revoke such election after the initial benefit payment has been
drawn by the State Treasurer .
(4) The member may elect to receive an annual retire-
ment allowance , payable monthly during his lifetime , equal
to one and three- fourths percent of final compensation for each
year of service provided that such monthly payments shall be
reduced by eighty - five dollars beginning with the first payment
following his sixty-fifth birthday .
(5) The member may elect to receive a decreased level
monthly retirement allowance payable for life, actuarially
equivalent to the retirement allowance payable under subsection
(4) of this section .

to be known as The Breaks Interstate Park, which shall be lo-

River and on adjacent areas in Pike County , Kentucky , and Dick-

such area and of such character as may be determined by the
