classification.title: An Act relating to professional boxing and wrestling matches and exhibitions.
classification.summary: This statute requires individuals conducting professional boxing or wrestling matches or exhibitions to furnish a written report to the commission within twenty-four hours of the event's termination. This report must detail the number of tickets sold, gross receipts, and other information prescribed by the commission. A tax of five percent of the gross receipts from ticket sales is also mandated. Additionally, a five percent tax on gross receipts from all other sources, including broadcasting and television rights, is required. Prior to any match or exhibition, copies of contracts involving contestant compensation and contracts under which the organizer receives compensation must be filed with the commission. The statute also outlines procedures for the commission to examine books and records if reports are not made or are unsatisfactory. Finally, suppliers of radio, television, or cable facilities must notify the commission prior to the contest.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the regulation and taxation of professional boxing and wrestling matches. It outlines reporting requirements, tax obligations on gross receipts, and contract filings for organizers. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as a chapter from a legislative session (Gen Assemb 1964.pdf).

entries.ocr_text:
620
ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY

**Section 17.** No boxing or wrestling match or exhibition shall be of more than twelve rounds in length except championship matches, which shall be limited in the discretion of the commission.

**Section 18.** Contestants in a professional or amateur boxing or wrestling match or exhibition shall be examined by a reputable licensed physician appointed by the commission, and shall be in excellent health before participating in such a boxing or wrestling match or exhibition.

**Section 19.** Decisions may be rendered in any boxing or wrestling match or exhibition, permitted by this chapter, in the discretion of the commission and by such method as it by rule prescribes.

**Section 20.** All structures or parts of structures used, or intended to be used, for boxing and wrestling matches and exhibitions, shall be properly ventilated and provided with fire exits and fire escapes, if need be, and shall conform to the laws, ordinances and regulations pertaining to buildings in the city where situated.

**Section 21.** No contestant under eighteen years of age shall be knowingly issued a license or allowed to participate in any professional boxing or wrestling match or exhibition, nor shall any person obtain, or cause to be obtained, a license to any person under eighteen years of age, to participate in any professional boxing or wrestling match or exhibition.

**Section 22.** All peace officers, on being informed or having reason to believe that a professional match that is not authorized under this chapter is about to take place, or that there is training or preparation for such a contest, in any place within their jurisdiction, shall suppress and prevent it.

For this purpose any peace officer may enter any place where such contest is being held or will be held or where there is training or preparation for such a contest and may arrest without a warrant any person who does not submit satisfactory proof that he has whatever license or permit is required by this chapter.

be set for a date not less than ten nor more than thirty days from
the filing of the notice , and written notice of the time and place
of the hearing shall be mailed to the appellant not less than five
days in advance of such hearing . Upon the mutual agreement of
the commission and appellant an earlier or later date may be
fixed , in which event no notice to appellant shall be required .
All hearings shall be held at the office of the commission
unless in the judgment of the commission it will be more expedi-

CHAPTER 173 629
CHAPTER 172
(Н. В. 422 )
AN ACT relating to property taxes .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
Section 1. Personal property of non -residents of the state
in their original package or fungible goods in bulk , belonging to
a non-resident of the state , shipped into this state and placed in
a public warehouse for the purpose of trans-shipment to an out-
of-state or within -the- state destination and so designated on the
original bill of lading , or personal property of residents of the
state in their original package and fungible goods in bulk , belong-
ing to a resident of the state , placed in a public warehouse for
the purpose of trans-shipment to an out -of-state destination and
so designated on the original bill of lading , shall , while so in the
original package , or as fungible goods in bulk, in such warehouse ,
be subject to an annual ad valorem tax to the state equal to five
cents upon each one hundred dollars of the fair cash value of such
personal property . No portion of a premises owned or leased by
a consignor or consignee , or a subsidiary of a consignor or con-
signee , shall be deemed to be a public warehouse within the
meaning of this Act despite any licensing as such .
Section 2. No other tax shall be assessed by the state or
any county , city or other taxing district on such personal prop-
erty , or against the owner with respect thereto , while such prop-
erty is in the original package , or as fungible goods in bulk in
such warehouse .
Signed March 21 , 1964
CHAPTER 173
(Н. В. 435 )
AN ACT relating to a city/county children's home .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :

classification.title: AN ACT relating to property taxes.
classification.summary: This act establishes an annual ad valorem tax of five cents per one hundred dollars of fair cash value on personal property of non-residents and residents of Kentucky that is shipped into the state and placed in a public warehouse for trans-shipment, provided it remains in its original package or as fungible goods in bulk. It also stipulates that no other taxes shall be assessed by the state or any local taxing district on such property while it is in the warehouse under these conditions. Portions of premises owned or leased by consignors or consignees are explicitly excluded from being considered public warehouses.

Section 1. KRS 201.010 is amended to read :
As used in this chapter unless the context otherwise requires :
( 1 ) " Home " means the institution to be established and
maintained in any county having a city of the first class , in con-
formity with the provisions of this chapter .
( 2 ) "Board " means the body created by KRS 201.020 .
[ ( 3 ) " Dependent " , "neglected " and "delinquent children "
have the same meaning as in KRS 199.010 . ]
Section 2. KRS 201.020 is amended to read:
( 1 ) In each county containing a city of the first class there
shall be a bipartisan board known as the " Board for ( name of
city ) and ( ------- ) County Children's Home ” .
( 2 ) The board shall be composed of 14 [ 10 ] members ,
at least two of whom shall be women , appointed jointly by the
Mayor of the City and the County Judge of the County . Each
member shall be at least twenty - five years of age and a bona fide
resident of the county . The members shall be appointed in
equal proportion from the two political parties casting the high-
est and next highest number of votes at the last preceding gen-
eral election in the county .
( 3 ) The members of the board shall be divided into four
groups , of which two groups shall consist of three members each
and two groups shall consist of four members each . Members
shall serve for four years each , and the terms of the members of
any one group shall expire at the end of a year which is dif-
ferent from the year in which the terms of the members of any
other group expire . Members originally appointed during the
year following the effective date hereof may be appointed for
such terms as may be necessary to effectuate the rotation pro-
vided for in the preceding sentence .
[ ( 3 ) Three members of the board shall serve for two years ,
three for three years , and four for four years each. Members ap-
pointed after the original appointments shall serve for four years
each . The board shall be continuously divided between the two
major political parties as provided in sub-section ( 2 ) of this
section . ]

classification.summary: This act amends KRS 201.010 and KRS 201.020. It redefines terms used in the chapter and modifies the composition and appointment process for the board of a children's home in counties with a first-class city. The previous definition of 'dependent', 'neglected', and 'delinquent children' is removed, and the board size is increased from 10 to 14 members. The appointment process remains bipartisan and requires members to be residents of the county and at least 25 years old. The term lengths and rotation of board members are also adjusted.
classification.reasoning: The statute amends existing Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) related to children's homes. It focuses on the administrative aspects of establishing and managing such institutions, including definitions and board composition. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The changes are administrative and procedural, not discriminatory in nature. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: KRS 201.030 and KRS 201.040 Amendments
classification.summary: This statute amends KRS 201.030 and KRS 201.040. KRS 201.030 outlines the organization of a board, the election of officers, the selection and compensation of a superintendent for a home, and the employment of other staff. KRS 201.040 establishes the board as a corporation with corporate powers and grants it the authority to make rules and regulations for the management of the home, including the care, maintenance, training, education, discipline, discharge, release, and parole of children received into the home's care. The amendments focus on procedural aspects of board organization, superintendent and staff management, and the board's regulatory powers concerning children in its care.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains amendments to Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) 201.030 and 201.040. These statutes deal with the organization and powers of a board managing a 'home' for children, including the appointment of a superintendent and staff, and the rules for the care and management of the children. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The language used is procedural and administrative, focusing on the governance of the institution and the welfare of the children under its care. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 201.080 and KRS 201.090 regarding child welfare homes
classification.summary: This statute amends KRS 201.080 and KRS 201.090. KRS 201.080 is amended to allow the board to receive and maintain any boys under 17 and girls under 18 who are residents of the county and are either duly committed to the home's care or found to be in the best interest of the child by the board, superintendent, or designated officers upon voluntary application. KRS 201.090 is amended to state that the board may file a petition when it has probable cause to believe a child under 17 or 18 is abandoned, neglected, cruelly treated, habitually in public places, or lives by begging.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the care and commitment of children to a home, amending existing statutes related to child welfare. There is no explicit or implicit mention of race or any discriminatory provisions based on race. The amendments focus on the age of children, residency, and the conditions under which they can be received into care, as well as the process for initiating legal action for neglected or abandoned children. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as part of the 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY 1964'.

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 201.100 and KRS 201.110 concerning child custody and commitment
classification.summary: This statute amends KRS 201.100 and KRS 201.110. KRS 201.100 outlines the process for filing a petition in county court regarding a child who comes under the provisions of KRS 201.090 (which lists conditions for a child to be considered delinquent or in need of supervision, such as associating with corrupting influences, being vicious or incorrigible, or having parents with habits of drunkenness or unlawful cohabitation). The court issues a writ for the child's custody, and notice of the hearing is served on parents or guardians. If the petition's facts are found true, the court commits the child to the board's custody and may require parents to pay for the child's support. KRS 201.110 states that children received into the home's care by court commitment remain under its care for a specified period.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with child welfare and the legal process for handling children deemed delinquent or in need of supervision. It outlines procedures for court intervention, custody, and commitment. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against a specific racial group. The conditions for intervention are based on behavior and parental circumstances, not race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 201.120 and KRS 201.130
classification.summary: This statute amends KRS 201.120 to change the board's discretion in providing education and vocational training for children in their care, allowing them to 'provide for' rather than 'shall provide for and maintain' schools and instruction, and to 'place' rather than 'bind out' children as apprentices. It also amends KRS 201.130 regarding the board's duty to protect children with less severe behavioral problems from those with more serious issues.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an amendment to existing statutes (KRS 201.120 and KRS 201.130) concerning the care and education of children under the supervision of a board. The language used focuses on the board's discretionary powers regarding education, vocational training, and the placement of children as apprentices. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The changes are procedural and administrative, related to the management of children's welfare and development. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the classification and segregation of inmates of the home for delinquent children.
classification.summary: This act mandates the classification and segregation of inmates in a home for delinquent children based on race, sex, and the nature of their delinquency or conviction. It also emphasizes preventing association between 'good' and 'bad' conduct children.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mandates segregation 'according to race' for inmates in a home for delinquent children. This is a clear indicator of Jim Crow-era practices, which enforced racial segregation in various institutions. The category is 'incarceration' because it pertains to the management of individuals within an institutional setting, even if it's a home for delinquents rather than a traditional prison. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as an Act passed by the General Assembly in 1964. The confidence is high due to the explicit mention of racial segregation.

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 107.140(2) regarding assessment of benefitted property
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 107.140(2) of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to clarify the assessment and payment of benefitted property taxes. It specifies how property owned by cities, counties, the state, and the U.S. Government should be assessed and paid for, with particular attention to state-owned property and the appropriation of funds for such assessments. It also mentions property vested in the Commonwealth for the benefit of a district Board of Education.
classification.reasoning: The statute amends an existing section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS 107.140) concerning the assessment and payment of taxes on benefitted property. The text focuses on how different governmental entities (city, county, state, U.S. Government) and specific educational entities should be assessed and how payments should be made. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language is purely administrative and financial regarding property taxation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: CHAPTER 176
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for state printing contracts in Kentucky. 642 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
Section 1. A new section of Chapter 421 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is created to read as follows :
( 1) Each circuit clerk shall pay to each witness the compen-
sation allowed to him by law, under appropriations provided by
the General Assembly and in accordance with procedures govern-
ing expenditures from appropriations . He shall take a receipt for
each payment made by him showing the date on which payments
is made . For the purpose of providing for such payments , the
State Treasurer , on direction of the Department of Finance , shall
advance to each circuit clerk an imprest fund sufficient for the
payment of the fees of all Commonwealth's witnesses for a term
of court or , if the court is one of continuous session , sufficient for
a period of time not to exceed sixty days .
( 2 ) The circuit clerk shall be allowed an amount equal to
five percent of the funds disbursed by him to witnesses under
this section .
Section 2. Subsection ( 1) of Section 421.020 of the Ken-
tucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows :
Witnesses subpoenaed for the state in felony cases shall be
allowed [out of the State Treasury ] one dollar per day for each
day in attendance in the circuit court , beginning with the day
set for the trial of the case . If a witness resides in another county
he shall receive in addition to the per diem , four cents per mile
going and returning by nearest traveled route to be allowed by
the circuit court and certified to the circuit clerk [Department
of Finance ] for payment .
Section 3. Section 421.030 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows :
A Commonwealth's witness in a felony case who resides in
another state shall be allowed his necessary expenses in addition
to the per diem , but no such allowance shall be made unless the
judge of the court has made an order based upon the personal
knowledge of the circuit judge or upon information showing that
the testimony of the witness is material to the state , requiring
the attendance of the witness . The expense and per diem shall
be allowed by the circuit court and certified to the circuit clerk
[Department of Finance ] for payment .

It also clarifies the process for approving and certifying these payments.

AN ACT relating to the powers of cities of the third, fourth , fifth and
sixth classes to condemn property for sewerage systems and sewage

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 94.170 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is hereby amended to read as follows :
Whenever any city of the third, fourth , fifth or sixth class
determines to acquire or construct a sewerage system under the
provisions of KRS 94.160 , and the city cannot agree with the
owner as to the compensation for the sale of any property located

publishing an ordinance declaring the necessity, condemn and
take possession of the property for that purpose in the manner
provided in KRS 416.010 to 416.080 .

vertently omitted from the provisions of this section and in part
now face the necessity of availing themselves of the provisions
hereof , an emergency is declared to exist and this act shall become
effective upon
itspassage and approval by the Governor

classification.title: An Act relating to fire protection districts
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for establishing, modifying, and defining the boundaries of fire protection districts and volunteer fire department districts in Kentucky. It specifies procedures for petitions, objections, and court judgments. A key provision (Section 1(2)) states that no fire protection district shall include territory within a city that maintains a 'regular fire department' and has paid its proportionate share of indebtedness for that territory. Section 2 amends KRS 75.020 regarding the enlargement or diminishment of such districts.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to the establishment and management of fire protection districts. It details legal procedures for creating, expanding, or shrinking these districts and includes a clause about their relationship with incorporated cities that have their own fire departments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language and subject matter are purely administrative and logistical concerning public services. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

department district , and the court shall promptly set the case
for a hearing . If upon such hearing , the court finds from the evi-
dence that a failure to annex or strike off such territory will
materially retard the functioning of the fire protection district
or the volunteer fire department district and materially affect
adversely the owners and the inhabitants of the territory sought
to be annexed or stricken off, it shall enter a judgment , granting
the annexation or striking off the territory . In the latter event ,
no new petition to annex or strike off all or any part of the same
territory shall be entertained for a period of two years . The
pleadings and practice herein provided shall be the same as

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 75.070 and 75.080 regarding fire departments
classification.summary: This statute amends sections of the Kentucky Revised Statutes related to fire departments. It clarifies that fire departments (municipal, fire protection district, and volunteer) and their personnel are considered agents of the Commonwealth when performing emergency services, both inside and outside their designated areas. This designation grants them immunity from liability for damages due to omissions or acts of commission or negligence while responding to alarms, performing fire prevention, or other authorized emergency services. It also addresses the maintenance of water pipes in fire protection districts.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the legal status and liability of fire departments and their personnel. It focuses on governmental immunity and the scope of their operations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the appointment of Judges Pro Tem of Police Courts in cities of the third class.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 26.250 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to allow the judge of a police court in a city of the third class to appoint a standing judge pro-tem. This pro-tem judge will serve at the will of the regular judge in the regular judge's absence or disability, exercising the same powers, performing the same duties, receiving prorated compensation, and having the same qualifications as the regular judge. The compensation of the pro-tem judge will not be deducted from the regular judge's salary.

Neither of these acts contains any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. They are administrative and financial in nature and do not appear to be related to Jim Crow laws. Therefore, 'is_jim_crow' is 'no', 'category' is null, and 'racial_indicator' is 'none'.

classification.summary: This section of the 1964 Acts of the General Assembly of Kentucky details the authorization and management of bond issuance for construction projects. It outlines the process for the State Property and Buildings Commission to issue General Construction Bonds and Highway Construction Bonds, including the segregation of funds, expenditure procedures, and investment of proceeds in U.S. Government securities. classification.title: CHAPTER 188
classification.summary: This statute, Chapter 188 from the 1964 legislative session, establishes the "State Highway Construction Fund of 1965" from the proceeds of Highway Construction Bonds. It outlines the expenditure of these funds for highway, bridge, and tunnel construction or reconstruction, particularly where federal cost participation is involved. It also details the process for defraying expenses related to bond issuance. The statute further designates these bonds as direct general obligation bonds of the Commonwealth, backed by its full faith, credit, resources, and unlimited taxing power. It mandates the annual levy and collection of taxes sufficient to pay interest and discharge the debt within thirty years, specifying that taxes on real, tangible personal, and intangible personal property will be used for this purpose, with proceeds appropriated to the "General Construction Bonds Sinking Fund."

It outlines financial mechanisms, taxation, and fund management. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. It outlines the allocation of bond proceeds for highway construction/reconstruction and for state agencies and public purposes, including the acquisition of lands, construction of buildings, and purchase of equipment for various state institutions and departments. It also specifies the wording for a ballot question regarding the approval of these bonds for a General Election in November 1965.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the financial mechanisms for funding infrastructure and state agency projects through bond issuance. It specifies the amounts allocated for highways and various state institutions like the Kentucky Industries for the Blind, School for the Deaf, School for the Blind, and several state colleges. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it refers to an 'Act of the General Assembly' from a specific year (1964) and is part of the 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY'.

classification.title: CHAPTER 188
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for submitting provisions of an Act to the people of the Commonwealth for a general election on November 2, 1965. It details the requirements for the Attorney General to prepare a "Notice of Election" and for the Secretary of State to advertise this notice in five daily newspapers across the Commonwealth. The notice must include the full ballot question and the provisions of the Act. The statute also specifies the timing and format of these advertisements, as well as the payment and proof of publication requirements for the newspapers.

classification.title: An Act relating to the issuance and sale of bonds by the State Property and Buildings Commission
classification.summary: This statute details the procedures and conditions for the issuance and sale of bonds by the State Property and Buildings Commission. It covers aspects such as bond dating, maturity, interest rates, sale price, denominations, redemption, payment locations, and the bidding process for bond sales. The act specifies that bonds shall be sold for not less than par and at prevailing maximum interest rates, with interest payable semi-annually. It also outlines the requirements for public notice and sealed bids for bond sales, and reserves the right for the Commission to reject bids.

classification.title: ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
classification.summary: This section of the Acts of the General Assembly of 1964 details the exemption of certain bonds and their interest from taxation by the Commonwealth and its political subdivisions. It outlines the procedures for signing, sealing, and validating these bonds, including provisions for facsimile signatures and the replacement of damaged or lost bonds. It also specifies that bonds can be issued without additional consent from other government entities. Section 10 defines the fiscal year of the Commonwealth.

CHAPTER 189

AN ACT relating to absentee voting .
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky :
Section 1. Section 126.140 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows :
As used in this chapter , unless the context otherwise requires :
(1 ) "Absent voter " means any qualified voter [in the
United States services , or bona fide , full time student in residence
at an educational institution ] who expects to be absent from the
county in which is situated his voting precinct on the day of
holding any election .
( 2 ) "Election ” means any regular election , primary election
or special election , for any federal , state , district , county or city
office , including school district offices , and any election upon a
public question .
( 3 ) "Mail " means the delivery to a mail carrier or the
deposit in a letter box or post office of a letter , with postage
prepaid.
(4) For the purpose of absentee registration , " United
States services " means :
( a) Members of the armed forces while in the active
service , and their spouses and dependents residing with them .
(b) Members of the merchant marine of the United States ,
and their spouses and dependents residing with them.

side of the continental United States , ] in

classification.title: AN ACT relating to absentee voting.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 126.140 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to expand the definition of "absent voter" to include individuals in United States services, their spouses and dependents, and civilian employees of the United States residing outside the continental United States, along with their spouses and dependents. It also includes members of religious or welfare agencies assisting the armed forces. The act clarifies the definition of "election" and "mail."
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns absentee voting and expands eligibility for certain groups, particularly those in military service and their families, and civilian employees abroad. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that would implicitly discriminate based on race. The changes appear to be administrative or to accommodate specific circumstances related to service and employment, not to enforce racial segregation or disenfranchisement. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Section 5. KRS 126.170 is amended to read:
( 1 ) Upon receipt of any application for an absent voter's
ballot, the county court clerk shall examine the registration books
for the purpose of ascertaining whether or not the applicant is a
registered voter as stated in the application . If the county court
clerk finds that the applicant is so registered , he shall , subject to the
provisions of Section 7 of this Act , mail a ballot to the voter as pro-
vided in KRS 126.190 . [ If the clerk finds that this applicant is not
registered , he shall upon being satisfied that the applicant is en-
titled to be registered, cause the applicant to be registered in the
registration records of the proper precinct , and shall mark upon
such registration records the words "Absentee Registration ."
Such registration may be made not later than twenty days before
the election for which an absent voter's ballot is applied for. ]
( 2) Any person who has been registered under the pro-
visions of Section 2 of this Act shall be required to reregister in
person upon his first return to the county of his residence at a
time when the books are open .
( 3 ) The oath required of a member of the United States
services may be administered and attested by any commissioned
officer in the active service of the armed forces , or any member
of the merchant marine of the United States designated for this
purpose by the Secretary of Commerce , or any civilian official
empowered by state or federal law to administer oaths .
Section 6. KRS 126.210 is amended to read :
( 1 ) The Secretary of State shall cause to be prepared and
printed an appropriate number of inner envelopes for absent

Section 7. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows :
Beginning on the nineteenth day prior to the election during
the hours in which the county court clerk's office is open , the
county board of election commissioners and the county clerk ,
together with interested candidates or their representatives , and
representatives of news media , shall examine all applications for
an absent voter's ballot for the purpose of determining the voter's
right to make application , as well as the validity of the form and
method of submitting the application itself . The county clerk
shall have the registration records at the meeting . The county
clerk shall read the name of each applicant aloud , and the
applicant and his application may be challenged by anyone
present . If the applicant or his application is challenged , the
board shall check the applicant's signature with the signature on
the registration record . The board shall determine whether or
not each challenged applicant shall be accepted or rejected .
If the application is rejected it shall be so marked and the voter
notified of this fact and the reason therefor . No ballot shall be
sent in response to a rejected application or prior to the examina-
tion provided for in this section .
( 2 ) The provisions of subsection ( 1 ) of this section shall
not apply to applications submitted by members of the United
States services .
( 3 ) The county clerk shall , however , retain the envelope
or post card used by any member of the United States services
for inspection and examination ,

As used in this Chapter unless the context requires otherwise ;
(1) "Board" means the Workmen's Compensation Board ;

pensation Board .
Section 2. Section 342.005 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows :
( 1) This chapter shall apply to all employers having three
or more employes regularly engaged in the same occupation or
business , and to their employes , except that it shall not apply
to domestic employment , agriculture , steam railways , or such
common carriers other than steam railways for which a rule of
liability is provided by the laws of the United States ; provided ,
however , it shall apply to the operators of threshing machines
used in threshing or hulling grain or seeds . It shall affect the
liability of the employers subject thereto to their employes for
a traumatic personal injury sustained by the employe by acci-
dent , and for disability resulting from occupational diseases as
defined in this chapter , arising out of and in the course of his
employment , or for death resulting from such accidental injury
or occupational disease ; provided , however , that "traumatic per-
sonal injury by accident " as herein defined shall not include
diseases except where the disease is the natural and direct result
of a traumatic injury by accident nor shall it include the results
of a pre -existing disease , whether previously disabling or not ,
but shall include injury or death due to inhalation in mines of
noxious gases or smoke , commonly known as "bad air " , and also
shall include injuries or death due to the inhalation of any kind of
gas , and shall also include occupational disease as defined in this
chapter .
( 2 ) The board shall apportion the aggregate extent and
duration of disability among the contributing causes including ,
but not limited to , the following :
( a ) "Traumatic injury by accident " ;
( b ) Pre -existing disease previously disabling ;
( c ) Pre-existing disease not previously disabling but
aroused into disabling reality by the injury or occupational disease .

classification.summary: This statute amends KRS 342.010 and KRS 342.020, which pertain to workers' compensation. It clarifies the definition of 'employer' to include municipal corporations and their subdivisions, and specifies how elections regarding coverage are exercised by governing bodies. It also addresses coverage for state employees and notes that the chapter does not amend or repeal statutes related to relief, pensioning, retirement, or other benefits for municipal or state employees and their dependents. The amendments focus on the scope of coverage and administrative procedures for workers' compensation claims.

classification.title: An Act to amend KRS 342.080 and KRS 342.095 relating to workmen's compensation.
classification.summary: This act amends sections of the Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning workmen's compensation. It clarifies the definition of dependency, specifies how periods of disability are deducted from death benefits, sets minimum and maximum weekly death benefits, and outlines conditions under which compensation to dependents ceases, including marriage, reaching the age of twenty (or twenty-five if enrolled in full-time education), or incapacitation from wage earning. It also details how remaining compensation is recalculated when one dependent's benefits cease. Additionally, it addresses the employer's obligation to pay weekly benefits for total disability, excluding the first seven days.

classification.title: Section 342.100 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is revised to read as follows
classification.summary: This statute revises the compensation for temporary partial disability resulting from an occupational disease. It outlines the weekly compensation as sixty-six and two-thirds percent of the difference between average weekly earnings before the injury and what the employee earns or is able to earn during the disability. It also sets limits on the duration of compensation (not to exceed four hundred weeks) and the maximum weekly and total sums. It further specifies that the maximum basic weekly income benefits for temporary partial disability shall not exceed 50% of 85% of the average weekly wage of the state. Finally, it states that if partial disability follows a period of total disability, the period of total disability will be deducted from the maximum period allowed for partial disability.

It also mentions provisions for lump sum payments and the shortening of weekly payments if they fall below a certain amount.

CHAPTER 192 703
(a) If at the time of the injury which resulted in death or
disability or the last date of injurious exposure preceding death
or disability from an occupational disease :
1. The wages were fixed by the week , the amount so fixed
shall be the average weekly wage;
2. The wages were fixed by the month , the average weekly
wage shall be the monthly wage so fixed multiplied by twelve and
divided by fifty-two ;
3. The wages were fixed by the year, the average weekly
wage shall be the yearly wage so fixed divided by fifty - two ;
4. The wages were fixed by the day , hour , or by the out-
put of the employe, the average weekly wage shall be the wage
most favorable to the employe computed by dividing by thirteen
the wages (not including overtime or premium pay) of said em-
ploye earned in the employ of the employer in the first, second ,
third, or fourth period of thirteen consecutive calendar weeks in
the fifty - two weeks immediately preceding the injury .
5. The employe had been in the employ of the employer
less than thirteen calendar weeks immediately preceding the
injury , his average weekly wage shall be computed under para-
graph (4), taking the wages (not including overtime or premium
pay ) for such purpose to be the amount he would have earned had
he been so employed by the employer the full thirteen calendar
weeks immediately preceding the injury and had worked , when
work was available to other employes in a similar occupation .
6. The hourly wage has not been fixed or can not be as-
certained , the wage for the purpose of calculating compensation
shall be taken to be the usual wage for similar services where
such services are rendered by paid employes .
(b) In occupations which are exclusively seasonal and there-
fore cannot be carried on throughout the year, the average weekly
wage shall be taken to be one - fiftieth of the total wages which
the employe has earned from all occupations during the twelve
calendar months immediately preceding the injury .
(c) In the case of volunteer firemen , police , and civil de-
fense members or trainees , the income benefits shall be based on
the average weekly wage in their regular employment .

CHAPTER 192 705
subject employers under the Kentucky Unemployment Insurance
law for the preceding calendar year shall be divided by the aver-
age monthly number of insured workers (determined by dividing
the total number of insured workers reported for the preceding
year by twelve .) The average annual wage thus obtained shall be
divided by fifty - two and the average weekly wage thus deter-
mined rounded to the nearest cent . Such average weekly wage
shall be certified to the Director by the Department of Economic
Security in a manner prescribed by the Workmen's Compensation
Board by regulation . The average weekly wage as so determined
shall be applicable for the full period during which income or
death benefits are payable , when the date of occurrence of injury
or of disablement in the case of disease , or of death, falls within
the calendar year commencing January 1 following the September
1 determination . Whenever a change in the average weekly wage
of the state is of such amount that the minimum weekly income
benefits for total disability or for death are increased or decreased
by one dollar or more , or the maximum weekly income benefits
for total disability or for death are decreased by two
dollars or more, computed in each case and rounded to the
nearest dollar , an adjustment in those minimums or maximums
which are affected in the requisite amount by the change in the
average weekly wage of the state shall be made which will
reflect this increase or decrease , but no change in such limita-
tions shall otherwise be made .
[If the employe , at the time of the injury, is regularly em-
ployed in a higher grade of work or occupation than formerly
during the year , and with larger regular wages, only such higher
grade of work or occupation , if it is not seasonal , shall be
taken into consideration in computing his average weekly wages . ]

Section 19. Section 342.270 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows :
(1 ) If the parties fail to reach an agreement in regard to
compensation under this chapter , [or if they have previously filed
such an agreement with the board and compensation has been
paid or is due in accordance therewith and the parties there-
after disagree , ] either party may make written application to the
board for a hearing in regard to the matter at issue and for a
ruling thereon . Such application must be filed within one year
after the accident , or, in case of death , within one year after
such death , or within one year after the cessation of voluntary
payments , if any have been made .
(2) As soon as possible after the application has been re-
ceived the board shall set the date for a hearing , to be held as
soon as is practicable in view of the matter involved , and shall
notify the parties at issue of the time and place of such hearing.
(3 ) Unless otherwise agreed to by the parties and author-
ized by the board , the hearing shall be held at or convenient
to the place where the injury was sustained or the ground for
disagreement occurred . Before directing a hearing , the board ,
[or ] a member thereof [or ] the Director or a hearing officer
[referee ] authorized by the board , may confer informally with
the parties at issue in an attempt to assist in adjusting their dif-
ferences , but may not delay the granting of a hearing , over the
objection of either party , for such purpose .
(4) If the parties have previously filed an agreement which
has been approved by the board and compensation has been
paid or is due in accordance therewith and the parties thereafter
disagree either party may invoke the provisions of KRS 342.125
which remedy shall be exclusive .
(5 ) An application for adjustment of claim shall be held in
abeyance by the board during any period voluntary payments of
compensation are being made under any benefit sections of this
Act to the maximum which the employe's wages shall entitle him ,
unless it shall be shown that the prosecution of the employe's
claim would be prejudiced by delay .

classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for reviewing orders or awards made by the Workmen's Compensation Board. It details how a petition for review should be filed, the issuance of summons, the submission of the original record by the board, and the grounds upon which a circuit court can review the decision. The court's review is limited to specific criteria such as whether the board acted within its powers, if the award was procured by fraud, if it conforms to the chapter's provisions, if it's clearly erroneous based on evidence, or if it's arbitrary or capricious.

classification.title: Section 342.316 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 342.316 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to define 'occupational disease' and 'injurious exposure' for the purpose of workers' compensation. It clarifies that ordinary diseases of life are not compensable unless they follow as an incident of an occupational disease. The statute outlines the criteria for a disease to be considered arising out of employment, requiring a direct causal connection to the work conditions and exposure.

classification.title: An Act relating to occupational diseases and workmen's compensation.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and limitations for filing claims related to occupational diseases under the Workmen's Compensation Act. It specifies when notice of a claim must be given, the time limits for filing a claim, and conditions for claims related to silicosis, pneumoconiosis, and radiation disease. It also states that the amount of compensation will be the same as for accidental injury.

classification.title: An Act relating to Workmen's Compensation
classification.summary: This act amends the Workmen's Compensation Act to clarify provisions regarding compensation payments for occupational diseases, including silicosis and pneumoconiosis. It specifies the calculation of compensation dates, conditions for death benefits, requirements for autopsy testimony, and conditions under which compensation may be denied due to false representations or omissions by the employee regarding previous health or employment history. It also outlines how compensation should be adjusted when an occupational disease is aggravated by other conditions or vice versa.

It outlines procedures for compensation, conditions for eligibility, and factors affecting the amount of compensation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1964. The category is 'labor' as it pertains to worker's compensation.

classification.title: CHAPTER 192
classification.summary: This section of the statute, specifically subsections (10) through (13), deals with compensation for occupational diseases. It outlines conditions under which compensation is payable, including limitations on beneficiaries (e.g., no compensation to those whose relationship arose after the first compensable disability, except for after-born children of an existing marriage). It also addresses the amendment of claims, liability of employers for occupational diseases, and exceptions for silicosis. The text focuses on the legal and administrative aspects of workers' compensation for occupational illnesses.

classification.title: Amendment to KRS 278.010 concerning definitions for utilities
classification.summary: This statute amends Section 278.010 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to update the definitions of 'corporation', 'person', and 'utility'. It clarifies what entities are considered utilities, including those involved in electricity, gas, pipeline transport, water, and telecommunications. The amendment removes specific exclusions for water districts and cities from the definition of 'utility' in certain contexts and also removes the exclusion for street, suburban, or interurban railways.

It focuses on the operational and definitional aspects of utility services like electricity, gas, water, and telecommunications. There is no mention of race, and the definitions and amendments do not appear to have any discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it amends the Kentucky Revised Statutes.

classification.title: An Act relating to public utilities and the Public Service Commission.
classification.summary: This act amends Chapter 278 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes. It clarifies that water districts, combined water, gas, or sewer districts, and water commissions are considered public utilities subject to the Public Service Commission's jurisdiction. It also adjusts the maximum annual assessment for maintaining the Public Service Commission from $400,000 to $450,000, with a minimum assessment of $5 per utility.

The core of the amendment is to classify certain districts (water, gas, sewer) as public utilities and subject them to the Public Service Commission's jurisdiction. It also adjusts the funding mechanism for the commission. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to circuit courts of the Commonwealth of Kentucky.
classification.summary: This act amends the holding of circuit courts in counties with a specific type of town structure. It mandates that circuit courts be held alternately in the county seat town and a larger town within the county, provided certain conditions regarding population, distance from the county seat, and the larger town's willingness to bear expenses for court facilities are met. Civil and criminal cases are to be tried in the town nearest to where the defendant resides or the offense was committed, respectively.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the procedural aspects of holding circuit courts in specific county structures. It outlines how court terms should be divided between towns and where civil and criminal cases should be tried based on proximity. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The classification of towns and the determination of trial locations are based on population, distance, and the defendant's residence or location of the offense, not race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: AN ACT relating to entertainment booking agents.
classification.summary: This act defines 'entertainment' and 'booking agent' for the purposes of regulating entertainment booking agents in Kentucky. It specifies that entertainment includes musical and acting performances by individuals or groups, and an entertainer is anyone performing such services for pay. A booking agent is defined as any person, partnership, or corporation that contracts for these services.
classification.reasoning: The provided text contains two distinct legislative items. The first is a resolution authorizing a payment to an estate. The second is an Act (H.B. 508) defining terms related to entertainment booking agents. Neither of these items contains any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation, which are characteristic of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, the statute is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. entries.ocr_text:
CHAPTER 200 729
scheduling such services as to specific date, time , duration , and
location , of any entertainer as defined in subsection ( a) .
( c ) "Department " means the Department of Revenue .
Section 2. No person shall engage in the occupation of en-
tertainment booking agent until he has paid to the Department
of Revenue the fees prescribed in this section and obtained a
license therefor . Before booking entertainment in this state , each
person so engaged shall pay the following fees for the calendar
year or fraction thereof :
( a) An annual license fee of thirty -five dollars shall accom-
pany each application for a license or the renewal of a license, to
be known as a booking agent's license .
( b ) A performance bond for the amount of five thousand
dollars shall accompany each license application and shall be
posted with the Department of Revenue to insure the perform-
ance of entertainers whose services may be contracted for
through such booking agent .
(c) Any person operating as a booking agent or perform-
ing such services in this state without first having paid the fee
and obtained the license prescribed in this section shall be sub-
ject to a penalty of fifty percent of the amount of the license fee ,
in addition to the fee itself , and six percent interest until paid.
Additionally , any person operating as an entertainment booking
agent or performing such services in this state who willfully fails ,
neglects or refuses to make a report , or to pay the license fees
as herein prescribed , or who shall refuse to permit the depart-
ment to examine the books, papers and records of any promotion
shall be guilty of a misdemeanor, and shall be fined not less than
one hundred dollars nor more than one thousand dollars , or im-
prisoned for a term not to exceed six months , or both .
(d) Any booking agent shall pay to the adversely affected
party an indemnity to be specified by contract for any entertain-
ment not performed as specified by such contract . Failure of the
booking agent to pay such indemnity within sixty days shall be
cause for the Department of Revenue to declare as forfeit all or
that portion of the performance bond necessary to make such
indemnification .
Became law without signature April 1, 1964
